column1: Certainly! Below is a detailed timeline table for the Habsburg Monarchy Vampire Panic (1725-1734), including key incidents, publications, and figures involved:

column3: Description

In Serbia, Petar Blagojević allegedly returns from the dead, causing the deaths of nine villagers. His body is exhumed, showing no decomposition, and he is staked and burned. This becomes the first officially recorded vampire case in the Habsburg Monarchy.
Austrian officials document the Blagojević case in a formal report, increasing vampire hysteria.

In Meduegna, Serbia, Arnold Paole, a former soldier, is believed to have turned into a vampire after his death. Villagers claim he killed multiple people, leading to mass exhumations and staking of suspected vampires.
Reports of vampire activity spread across the Habsburg territories, causing concern among nobility and religious authorities.
11 bodies are exhumed and found in an undecayed state; official Austrian medical inspectors participate, confirming villagers' fears.
The Habsburg army sends officers and medical doctors to investigate vampire cases in Serbia. Their findings support the idea that corpses show abnormal preservation.
Austrian physicians submit another detailed report on vampire cases, further legitimizing vampire fears.
Philosophers and theologians begin debating vampires, with treatises discussing the theological and medical basis of the phenomenon.
The Habsburg Monarchy, alarmed by public hysteria, issues an official decree discouraging vampire exhumations and reinforcing Church doctrine against such superstitions.
